CURRENT AFFAIRS.

We missed “ Pink’s *’ first round of shops, but we
have the second instalment before us as we write.
When there is anything really new in commercial
enterprise, and certainly when there is anything new
in Irish industrial enterprise, these matters are very
fit subjects for journalism, and they unfortunately are
rather neglected by journalism in this country. At

" Christmas time a few columns about the shops, if done
tolerably well, is not out of place, but the shops as
 they are sometimes done brings Dublin journalism
into ridicule. ¥

Bungs and Pawns are ridiculous subjects for puffs.
““ Pink’s *’*man thus commences to moralise in a pill
shop:—‘‘ Some people may be tempted in the round
 of festivities during the holidays to partake of some
e good things which they know  do not agree with

"but they can fortify themselves against existing

““Pink ”’ Puffs we are told that ‘‘even early in the
day the pedestrian traffic overflowed on to the roadways
in several thoroughfares.”” The third paragraph is as
follows :—*‘ Make the home cheerful by purchasing the
bright burning coals of Messrs. S Tho PNl
puffer visited a bungery in Moore Street, where a cer-
tain ale ‘‘ may be had here in good condition.”” He
went to a pawn also, for we are informed that *“ special
facilities are offered to clients at Messrs. -, ab
which the highest advances are made on all classes of
property.””  Shortly after puffing Pawn the readers are
told that if they want to enjoy the Christmas party,
they must be able to dance, and hey, presto! the
“ Pink ”’ puffer tells of a place where they *‘ guarantee
proficiency, after a special course of easy- lessons, in
one week.”’ Oh, what a Press!

Poor Bonar the stupid and crass
Talls foul of the carrion class.
Alf T'ox in a fret
On Bonar won't bet;
He’s saying that Law is an ass.,

A policeman was recently transferred from Arklow
and there was a presentation at which several Bungs
assisted.  The following Bungs were present at the
ceremony :—Messrs. Waldron, Kinsella, T. Kavanagh,
and J. J. Kavanagh. The noble profession of Pawn-
broking was also represented in the person of a Mr.
Norris. A clerk recently imported from the North was
also there. There was, we understand, no purse of
sovereigns on this occasion: the ‘‘ peace officer” had
to be content with a mere clock!

The bobbies are using their station
For dancing and great recreation.
Some say it befalls
That after the balls
Bung gives the lock-up occupation.

It is not so long ago that one of the staff of the
Freeman. newspapers was hauled before a Dublin
magistrate by Pensioner Yeats for protesting against
the infamous ‘‘ Playboy.”’  Since then Pensioner
Yeats and his gang have hawked the anti-Irish thing
through part of the United States. The actors are
now oft to the States again, and we see that the
Telegraph has a grotesque puff of their departure.

The company who hawked the ‘‘Playboy ” in
America and in London—a pldy, for protesting agai

which one ©f ‘the Freeman Co.’s-own staff was sworn
against by Rensioner Yedts—are now puffed by
‘“Pink | “4As \we 'so often have 'to gy, ‘oh, what o
Press| TIndeell, the puff is®o silly thint we wonder if
some of ‘those mentioned in it have not cause for an
action for libel against our contemporary for holding
them up to public ridicule. _ For instance, ‘‘ Pink ™
says:—'‘ Mr. Commissioner Bailey was there pouring
out fervent wishes for the success of the tour.”” Does
not this picture the sage Land Commissioner in an
attitude that may detract from the respect with which
unsophisticated purchasing tenants ought to regard
him? “Pink’s "’ man, or else its compositor, got a
bit off his balance in one part, for we are told :—** The
click of the snapshotte ralmost got on one’s nerves.’’
This ‘“ Pink "’ man, who writes as if he were the mem-
ber of the staff who does the pufis of Pawn and Bung,
said he had to bow his head ‘“lest any unlovely
features might mar the picture.”” We thought so;
it is just ‘tame, self-depreciating and contemptible
fellows who could write like that who would spin these
silly words over the departure of the *‘ Playboy '’ gang.
And this *“ dramatic critic ** referred in his puft to the
Atlantic Ocean as ‘ the Herring Pond ’'—that is just
in the style of the Bung and Pawn puffer. Oh, what a
Press! By the way, Commissioner Bailey pouring out
fervent wishes might do for a cartoon, on a push.

Some johnnies around us, who know
As much about art as a crow,
Pretend to feel pain
That back to the Lane
His picture collection may go.

Some weeks ago we had some comments on Dun-
garvan District Council and its insurance. One state-
ment made at the meeting was that the ** Hibernian
effected re-insurances with German companies. This
statement was a definite one made by a representative
of a rival company, and the ‘‘ Hibernian”’ promptly
brought the matter to an issue. ' As a result the Gene-
ral Manager of the Hibernian Insurance Co. received
the following letter, dated November 17th, from Mr.
V. Kyrke, Manager of the Alliance Assurance Co., Ltd.,
Nassau Street, Dublin:—‘‘ Dear Sir,—In the course
of a discussion at the meeting of the Dungarvan -Rural
District Council on 8th October last on the subject of
the Fire Insurance of the Council, it was stated by a
representative of this company that the * Hibernian’
effected re-insurances with German companies. You
have informed us that the ‘ Hibernian’ do not place
re-insurances with any foreign companies, and we have
pleasure in withdrawing the incorrect statement, and
expressing our regret that it should have been inadver-
tently made.”’

The General Manager-and Secretary of the Hibernian
has forwarded a copy of that letter to the papers in the
Co. Waterford and also the following letter on behalf
of his own company :—‘‘ Dear Sir,—At a recent meet-
ing of the Dungaryan Union and Rural District Coun-
cil, a representative of the Alliance Assurance Com-
pany, Limited, made certain statements regarding this
office which were incorrect. We brought the matter
under the notice of this company and received from
them for publication a letter, copy of which is enclosed,
veknowledging that a mistake had been made. Copy
of this letter was duly forwarded to the Clerk of the

. Union with a request that same be read to his Board,
with a view of having it published in the Press. We
are unable to trace that our communication has either
been read to the Board or appeared in the Press, nor

There once was a Sourface of cheek,
Who had fifteen shillings a week;
He swore he would join
A war on the Boyne
Ere eat Irish Government leek.

Mr. Justice Dodd was the judge at the Munster As-
sizes, and in summing up in one case talked in a most
sectarian manner. He instituted a comparison—and
a most grotesque hand he made of the business—be-
tween Protestantism and the Universal Church. What
business had Judge Dodd to talk sectarianism from
the bench? Is it contempt of Cqurt to say that his
conduct constitutes contempt of the country. He is
reported as saying: ‘‘ He thought it was the boast of
their religion, which demanded more from the indivi-
dual man than the religion of the great majority of ‘the
country, ete.” We don’t suppose Judge Dodd would,
defend his position against our friend from Ballycastle
if he were challenged to controversy. He further is
reported as having said: ““ Tha Protestant religion, if
it were worth anything, would Lo the religion of the
Good Samaritan, and, etc.”” We will not discuss these
points with Judge Dodd, but we protest against the
judicial bench being made a sort of street preacher’s
tub or an auxiliary to a Smyly ‘‘ bird’s nest.”” Judge
Dodd should stick to his last : he is highly paid for doing
very little; and he goes beyond his sphere when he
talks sectarianism from the Beneh. If a Catholic
judge—but we suppose all, or nearly all, Catholic judges
in Ireland are tame, so the supposition is rather wild—
were to tall from his religious point of view in a similar
way what an outery there would be.

This Judge Dodd appears to have had an edge on
him for interfering in other people’s business.
demned the Clare farmers for their alleged want of
generosity in supporting the Church—of which he is
not a member—and his Lordship the Most Rev. Dr.
Toley felt compelled to intervene and characterise this
Judge’s remarks in‘the matter as ‘“ unfounded, un-
called for and offensive.’’ Judge Dodd had better
be more caveful at the next Assizes.

A puffer who saw in the street
An ass that a carrot did eat,
Described it with force,
And so in due course
Came out a Stop Press on the feat.

We don’t suppose the slanderers who made such
wicked and lying use of the Castledawson row will even
foel ashamed, not to say apologiss, nobwithstanding
the remarks of Judge Wright’s summing up.
Lordship said he was glad to know that women and
children had not been struck by pikes. He did not
know whether the newspapers had headed their reporst
of this affair as an aftack upon women and children,

He was glad to say he had not read any of them. At
any rate, what had been absolutely proved in ‘that
court was that no injuries had been inflicted on any
woman -or child, and he was glad to hear that proved
in court: Tt detracted very much from the seriousness
of the charge, and they were glad to think that nothing
so dastardly or unmanly had taken place. The idea
~that decent, respectable-looking men like the prisoners
would attack a woman or a child was a horrible idea.
*‘ T am glad that the lie has been given to that,” added
his lordship.

There once was a Post Office gent
‘Who had a ““ Saved *’ temperament.
He started for bread
Where Norway is head,
And soon up the ladder was sent.

“ Pink *’ is only in the back seats compaved with
a Midland weekly in the shop-puffing line. O’l'ar-
rell’s, of Strokestown; now, where’s your Tommy
Lipton! We read:—'‘ What a place Ilphin Street
would be without O’Farrell’'s! It is well known as
the moon, just as full and as popular as a racecourse.
Once a customer here a customer for ever. A big trade
is glonc in the provision and grocery counters, and his
teas are splendid. The bar is stocked with well-
matured whiskies, best brands of wines and liquors and
stout, and are always in excellent condition, and to add
to the flavour you are bound to hear one or two ‘ good
({nes’ from the versatile proprietor while enjoying your
drink.”’

And then there are Sharkey Bros. We are told :—
‘“In Elphin Street Mr. Thomas Sharkey studies the
needs, the tastes, one might almost say the idiosyn-
crasies of his patrons, and in all the ramifications of
the drapery line the latest markets furnish the newest
fashions and the best and warmest things, Mr. Sharkey
sees that those who come to him will be provided for
and turned out in an up-to-date manner, so that an
attitude of perfect trustfulness produces a result as
satisfactory as. the sartorial knowledge of a Beau
Brummel.”

The Sharkey establishment is not all soft goods:—
““In Church Street Mr. J. J. Sharkey govern an em-
porium in which everything can be purchased. Fully
stocked and under a careful, assiduous and accom-
modating management, there is an assortment capable
of gratifying the most varied tastes, and a_knowledge
and a courtesy on the part of tic staff that cannot
fail to be helpful even to the self-reliant connoisseur.
At all times a source of attraction, Sharkey’s just now
is positively irresistible. His grocery, provision, and
bar are always well patronised, and then as he caters
for the wants of the farmers of the county in every
respect, his stores are worth inspecting, containing as
they do ironmongery, farm seeds and implements and
mowing machines.”’

In the great Ballypeelerstown hall

Miss Bung was the belle of the ball.
To the Pawn’s daughter there
She gave a cold stare,

And great was that lady’s downfall.

The “ Trish Times,”” of Saturday, had not the decency
to utter an editorial word about the Castledawson
affair. But in its Saturday’s issue it has a column
and a-half under the heading ‘‘ Christrnas Appeals.”

Father John O’Kelly, 609 So. Fourteenth Street,
Birmingham, Ala., U.S.A., in the course of a letter
dated December 6th, enclosing subscription for the
LEADER, remarks:—‘ The LEADER comes this distance
.regularly for a long time past. There is no more wel-
* come visitor. It invariably brings some good news.

* The number just to hand fells how the Freoman and
Independent devoted several columns each to the hurl-
ing at Jones’ Road, for instance. 'Tis good news, and
would not be suspected out here only for the LEAbER
says so. I hope the Freeman and Independent keep
up the good work, however, and continue to give ex-
tended accounts and pictures and premier position to
hurling as they ought to do in their sporting pages.

* Would not the sporting page on the daily LEADER

be worth while? Now, a teacher said to me recently
that you would have no such page in your paper, Mr.
Editor. T believe, however, that all these sports, even
horse-racing, can be treated from an Irish Ireland point
of view. Your editor in charge would set it forth, and
it would attract many young people, and get them in-
terested in other pages and departments in the daily
LeApER. Many of thesé young people are not reading
the present excellent little weekly. Inquiring the
reason I was told, as the most plausible one, that the
average education (say 4th to 6th grade National
school) was hardly equal to reading its well written
columns and articles intelligently. Yet it grips one so
and turns one Irish Irelandwards so surely that it is
a pity not to see it in evéry household in the country.
So I hope the daily, with all its departments and pages
appealing to as many as possible in Ireland, is not far
distant. The LeapER must do it, I believe. The pro-
vincial papers continue to publish Petty Sessions lists
and news in the old dull way, notwithstanding the
newly awakened interesting life all around them. I
spent a considerable time in Ireland this summer after
an absence of five years. Hopeful signs are numerous
but many of the people don’t see or realize them yet,
and are consequently in the same old ruts. One cause
of this is certainly the Press; they read the Dreadful,
the locals, and the English view point papers. One is
therefore glad to see the LEADER in many places doing
good work. T believe, as you have pointed out, you
get little help from many or some that ought to be
your friends. I called for the LEADER three times in
Gort (at Stephenson’s) before I got it.  First time
‘ extra copies had been returned,’” whatever that means;
second time, ‘sold out ’; third time I got it. I called
for it in several places at Messrs. Eason’s stalls. They
always had it, but in Queenstown, for instance, it had
to be dug up from beneath an armful of truly English
hodge-podge journals. At Enniskillen it had to be
gotten from somewhere underneath the counter. At
Tralee, however, the little LEADER was in honour af
Eason’s stall, and I could see it several yards down the
platform before I got to the stall itself. That’s the way
it ought to be at all Messrs. Eason’s stalls.

‘T fear, Mr. Editor, I have taken liberty with your
time and patience, but I hope my enthusiasm to see
the LEADER everywhere, and with everyone in Ireland,
if not also outside it will excuse me.2’

How would some of the provincial papers fill their
columns only for the drunkery cases. There were some
drunkery cases at the Tulsk (Co. Roscommon) Petty
Sessions. A man was prosecuted for falsely represent-
‘ing himself as a ‘‘bona fide.”” What a fierce light

THE LEADER.

sometimes beats up‘on a ““bona.”” The D. I said:
““ He was not (a bona fide), as he‘slfs’pt in a cock of hay
about a quarter of a mile from here.

A Bung named. P. Jameson—most appropriate name
—was charged with permitting drunkenness in his
bungery. There is a realistic and yet picturesque touch
about policemen’s evidence in drunkery cases that at
times suggests the Abbey Theatre ab its best. ~For in-
stance we read that Sergeant Begley deposed that on
the night of November 1st he was on duty ab defen-
dant’s house. He heard a step like that of a man
stumbling, and he listened a while, and In?\lnd a, young
man lying up against the wall, lighting a pipe. He was
speaking to another man, and actually glorying in being
drunk.  The boy was fined the last Court day for being
dvank. I went into the publichouse, and told the pub-
lican that a drunken man was after leaving his pre-
mises, but the defendant did not say anything. The
Bench was evenly divided, but the chairman was for
convicting the Bung, and the chairman remarked that
half of this drunkenness would not be known if the pub-
licans would refuse those men, who look intoxicated,
for a drink, but they simply give them drink as long as
they can stand up.

Kildare is one of the very few rotten Nationalist
County Councils whose University scholarships are
eligible in anti-Irish Trinity. We see by a public re-
port that one of its County Council scholars is in anti-
Irish Trinity studying—it’s little study he needs, says
you—to be a parson! By the way, Mr. Minch, of Kil-
dare, and one of the directors of the ‘‘ Freeman,’’ sent
one of his sons to Trinity last October. We will pro-
bably have more to say next week about the Kildare
County Council and Irish, and Trinity and Mr. Minch.

THE COMING OF HOME RULE. 3

JD BE political situation has taken a turn most
favourable to the passing of Home Rule. We
suppose strangers visiting Ireland are surprised at what
may strike them as a lack of political enthusiasm ; and
hostile critics may see, or pretend to see, a like apathy.
In fighting for Iiome Rule Irishmen are fighting for
something that they never experienced ; they are fight-
ing for something that experience has instinetly made
them regard as doubtful. The old man who said—
though the expression is somewhat in the form of a bull
—years ago, ' my opinion is that eventually we will
never get it,”’ echoes a sort of sub-conscious fear in
all our hearts. We will believe that we have Home
Rule when we have it. Disappointments have made
us sceptical.
- The power that we have in our County Councils
is not very much, but we have it and exercise
ib. Suppose that it were proposed to take that power
away and return to the old Grand Jury system, what
a fiery agitation would spring up all over the country.
We would be face to face with an effort to deprive us of
& power we have and that we use. It is not in human
nature to fight so keenly for the possession of what
you never had as to defend what you possess.  There
is no real apathy about Home Rule; but it is a thing we
have not yet possessed; it is a thing in the fight for
the possession of which we have been previously dis-
appointed ; and, at the present time, it is a fight in
which enthusiasm in Ireland does not appear to be
called for. We are intense Home Rulers, yet we talk
and write comparatively little about it. If Home Rule
were defeated this time, and the Unionists got into
power, there would be a great bursting out of Home
Rule passion and energy. We are rather cool and
quiescent now because we are waiting on events; be-
cause Home Rule is in the making, and there is a call
for quietness rather than for display; and because hav-
ing been previously disappointed, and the cup having
been dashed from our lips, the most optimistic of us
are not, to say the least, in a humour to prophesy
until we know. .

Our greatest negative asset, if we may use that
phrase, at the present time is the incompetence and
weakness of the Unionists, particularly of their third-
rate leader, Mr. Bonar Law. Bonar has done a good
service for Home Rule by practically splitting up the
Unionist Party. The poor ‘ Irish Times '’ provides
most enjoyable reading for us these days. It is cross
with Bonar! Here is how it commences its leading
article one day last week:— 2

The best proof that the Marquis of Lansdowne
. and Mr. Bonar Law have given their party a wrong
lead is the rejoicing of its enemies. The Ashton-
under-Lyne speech has transformed the political
situation. Depressed Liberals and silenced
Nationalists are again vocal and confident. The
glee of the official Nationalist Press is quite natural
—we should be gleeful if we were in its place. . . .
Party leaders are not infallible, and, when they
malke mistakes, the newspapers which are in sym-
pathy with their general policy ought to warn them
plainly and strongly of the nature and conse-
quences of those mistakes. We believe ourselves
to be acting in a spirit of true loyalty to the
Unionist Party when, speaking in the name of all
Trish Unionists, we deplore the substitution of a
definite and highly controversial policy of Tariff
Reform for Mr. Balfour’s equally straightforward,
but, far more simple and satisfactory, policy of the
referendum. ] :
Unhappy ““ Alf”’ is almost to. be pitied. . Listen to
thig:— :

The reforenduny is withdrawn, and Free Traders
who are otherwise in full agreement with the
Unionist programme have lost their only safeguard
against the introduction of a. drastic scheme of

CHRISTMAS BOXING.

HIS number of the LEADER should be in the
readers’ hands on Boxing Day. I would prefer -

to call this holiday St. Stephen’s Day, a title by which . .
it is better known in Ireland, were it not that the
theme on which T would dilate is more fittingly associ-
ated with the name I have chosen. That theme is
seasonable. It issad. Christmas Day has this pectli-
arity among festivals that while it has an'*‘ Eve >’ and
a_‘‘morning-after,”’ the festivity itself is, as it were, a
blank space between these two points of time. On

. Christmas Eve it is the pleasing practice of a large

scetion of the community to work themselves into a
state of excitement about the day to follow. On Box-
ing Day a feeling of flatness supervenes, caused for
the most part not by the rejoicing of the previous day,
but rather by the lack of it. True, there are maﬁy
who over eat, and mayhap over drink, on the 25th
December. These, of course, feal flat. Some, indeed,
malke a determined effort to oxtract from St. Stephen’s
Day the worldly entertainment they failed to find on
the previous day. But it may be taken as true that
)thle_ Christmas Eve represents the zenith of the

festive season,” Boxing Day is its nadir. To add

. lio the prevailing gloom let us discuss the Xmas Box

in its least pleasing forms and with all its attendant
horrors.

The subject is by no means a new one. Why do
people give Xmas Boxes at all? I may be said with
perfect truth that no one (not even the recipient) be-
lieves that it is more blessed to give than to receive.
The Christmas Box blesses neither him that gives nor
The donor growls at having to give

anything, the donee growls because the donation is not
enough. The sum bestowed taking, as it.usually does,
the form of silver coins, is almost always expended on
* the purchase of alcoholic stimulant, and thereby helps
to promote the curiously anti-Christian aspect which
Christmas is somewhat made to assume in these
islands.
. The real evil underlying Christmas Boxing, if I may
employ such a term, is that it is not, voluntary. The
Christmas Box, like the waiter’s tip, is not a free
gift, it is an enforced tax. In imagination, one fancies,
a cheerful giver seeking out a devoted and unassum-
ing servitor and, to his surprise and joy, forcing on his
reluctant acceptance a monetary token of appreciation
and regard. In reality, we have a truculent and
insolent person demanding a sum of mouney to which
he has no legal claim, and not concealing his disdain at
the paltry amount hes wrings from an unwilling donor.
Yet we are told by those ill-regulated and illusioned
people who gaze on the world through rose-hued glasses
that it is the beautiful spirit of peace and good-will
which actuates this giving and taking. It does not
conduce to peace; it Is not animated by good will,

Why, you say (although you really agree with me),
does this sour person diatribe thus? Why does he so
furiously rage, even if he does not imagine a vain
thing? The reason is so obvious that I will not with-
hold it. Because, of cowrse, I belong to the ranks of
those who give and do not get. T am one of the
““ boxers,”’ not one of the ‘“ boxed.”” Iest I cause
offence, and thereby cut-off the benefactions of those
who heap cigars, turkeys, motors, neckties and diamond
pins upon me at this season, let me hasten to explain
that I differentiate between the gifts of friends, and
the Xmas “‘ box *’ of commerce. Never shall I seek
to dry-up the wells of human affection which impell a
large section of mankind to lavish on me presents of
the kind T have enumerated. C’est une aulre chose,
as I learnt to say in the days of Compulsory French.
But do the employers who under-pay me during the
year hand me a bonus on the 25th December as a
Xmas Box? Does the editor of the T.EADER, his heart,
beating magnanimously after his Christmas dinner,
exclaim to me: ‘‘here’s a fiver for yourself as a pour
boire ”’ (he, too, is of my generation)?  Alas! no.
Does my grocer thrust old whiskey on me, or my

_ tobacconist choice Havanas? No, no. True, I once
had dealings with one of the latter class who, upon
Boxing Day, was wont to bestow on me a solitary
cigar. But in extenuation of his conduct it must be
pleaded that he was a foreigner, that he was a Jew—
and that the cigar was bad. In simple truth, the
Xmas Box, unlike the “ wran,”” cannot, on Stephen’s
Day, be caught by such as me in the furze or anywhere.

Time was when that shining trinity, the butcher, the
baker, the candle-sticker maker, despatched to their
customers at Yuletide tokens of ‘gratitude for custom
during thé past year. Doubts assail me as to the extent
to which the last-mentioned industrialist participated
in these doings, but we need not be too exact. The
emissaries who came to one’s home laden with these
free offerings-were rewarded by the grateful recipient
for their share in the kindly deed. Hence the Chyist-
mas Box, But all believers in the principle of ‘‘no
taxation without representation’’ may admit ‘Lhnt,
in this instance, we have now a taxation which is un-
deniably a tyranny. .

Some years ago the Law stepped in and put a stop
to certain forms of Christmas Box. It would be well—
for the ‘‘boved’’—if Hamlef’s advice had been fol-
lowed and the thing had been reformed altogether.
Perhaps, that may come later. Then there will be an

IRISH INNS.

l‘/I:\NY travellers to Ireland have in the written

accounts of their journeyings made allusions to
the hotels and inns where they have stayed. These
allusions have not always been flattering though often
amusing, and they have given rise to the idea that if
hospitality, originality, welcome, entertainment are to
be found in all Irish hostels, other things deemed indis-
pensable are sometimes lacking. No doubt these de-
scriptions have been coloured by the writers’ pens so
as to make good copy, but truth lurks below the ima-
ginings. Some of us have our own personal experiences
of lodgings in remote places whither business or a wan-
dering spirit led us. It requires no foreign traveller to
state that Irish inns—-leaving the larger hotels alto-
gether outside the question—might without becoming
luxurious be greatly improved.

Why is it that the very simple, very plain, very clean
little inns familiar to those who have visited the Con:
tinent are practically nowhere to’be found in Ireland ?
Such inns make ftravelling a pleasure, for it is possible
to go to the most out of the way place in security. It
is easy to conjure up the recollection of one of these
that exists in a little village of the Appenines a long
way from the railway station. The place is primitive
and is visited only by a few commercial travellers, an
artist, an occasional tourist. Like most Italian houses
of older date the floors are all of red brick.. There are
no carpets and two or three rugs take their place. The
diningroom on the right is very bare, containing only
the necessary furniture. On the left is the cabaret,
where the villagers come in the evening to sit in the
light and warmth and to discuss the events of the day.
No unpleasant smell of whiskey or porter greets the
tender nose of the traveller, for a very small glass of
absinth or some mild chianti are the popular drinks.
The guests are treated to a good and simple fare. There
is always a minestra—soup with vegetables, some
macaroni, risolto, gnocchi, or other such dish of the
country, chicken, cheese and fruit. In the morning
there is good coffee, fresh hread and butter. Upstairs
the bedrooms are plain but clean, and the beds invari-
ably comfortable owing to the Italian custom of teas-
ing the bedding yearly and of consfantly putting it out
in the sun. A strapping and talkative maid servant
in short skirt and linen blouse multiplies herself, and
everything is neat and clean. Other such inns exist
in I'rance, Germany, Switzerland, with the differences
between them of national characteristics. For they

are peculiarly national these hostels of the by-way, and
berein lies their great charm. They are not luxurious,
indeed they are sometimes austere, but the necessaries
are to be found if not the superfluities. Unlike the
great Anglo-American hotels that carry their own at-
mosphere with them to every place, these inns are of
the country, and its life can be studied in them.

Yet, curiously enough, Ireland before other countries
has a tradition on the matter of inns and places of lodg-
1})% The laws of ancient Ireland ordered the estab-
1is] ument, and upkeep of houses of hospitality, and the
:brughald]z, or hosteller, was a person of importance.
‘At the same time strict rules were laid down for him
tovfollo.w.. ‘“In order to be at all times ready to re-
ceive visitors,” to quote from Dr. Joyce, ‘‘a brughaidl
was bound to have three kinds of meat cooked and
ready to serve up to all who came; three kinds of raw
nieat ready for cooking; besides animals ready for kill-
ing.’” In one of the law tracts a brughaidh is described
as “‘a man of three snouts,” viz., the snout of a live
}mg rooting in the fields, the snout of a dead hog cook-
ing, and the pointed snout of a plough. . . . He
was also ‘““ & man of three sacks,”” for he had always
in his house a sack of malt for brewing ale, a sack of
salt for curing caftle joints, and a sack of charcoal for
the irons. 4 We are told that his kitchen fire
should be kept perpetually alight, and his caldron
never taken off the fire, and kept full of joints boiling
for the guests. There should be a number of open roads
leading to the house of a brughaidh, so that it might be
resdil{ accessible, and on each road a man stationed
to make sure that no traveller should pass by without
calling to be entertained ; besides there was a light to
be kept burning on the lawn at night to guide travel-
lers from a distance. The Irish missionaries
carried this fine custom to the Continent in early ages,
as they did many others; for we are told on the best
authority that before the ninth century they estab-
lished hostels chiefly for the use of pilgrims on their
way to Rome, some in Germany, but most in France,
as lying in the -direct route to the Kternal City.
Keating says there were ninety brughaidhs in Con-

- nacht, ninety in Ulster, ninety-three in Leinster, and
a hundred and thirty in Munster, all with open houses.

Such were the lodging houses of early Ireland. Their
manner of entertainment was lavish, not to say extra-
vagant, and in this they do not serve as good models
from an economic point of view.  Still there is a
medium, and between the fare prepared by the
brughaidh and that offered by the owner of a present
day Irish inn there js a great difference. Ireland is
literally a land ‘‘ flowing with milk and honey.”’ Irish
butter is considered the best in the market, and yet
what butter is often given to the traveller who comes
to a little country inn—and not only is the butter amiss
but the bread that comes from a stale loaf that has
been lying in the shop many hours. Then there is

. "American bacon to alternate with a rather tough mut-
" ton chop—and for vegetables, potatoes and cabbage.
‘A chicken is indeed a 7rara avis on the dinner table,
though there may be twenty live chickens scratching
round the door. The culinary mistress is not over-
burdened with imagination—and this want of imagina-
tion does not necessarily coincide with economy. The
sittingroom of the Irish inn is offen a veritable museum
—ornaments, albums, pictures, photographs, old
Christmas cards jostle each other. It is sometimes
difficult to find the space even to write a letter, or to
move a book without upsetting a knick-knack. Can
these things be dusted daily, or once a week, or once
a month ? It is mot possible. They are not works of
art, they fatigue the eye from their number and diver-
sity, and they leave no vacant place. A carpet covers
the floor to the wall end. What is under that carpet ?
When was it last shaken? It is better not to inves-
tigate these questions. As to the bedrooms, even in
them ornaments encumber the dressing table and the
mantelpiece. The carpet likewise gives no margin for
brush or duster. The bedding is often of feathers and has
" not been yearly teased or even sun-bathed. How one
. longs for the austerity of the little Italian inn where

the family have not spread their accumulated posses-
sions over the public sittingroom and where no dust
hides under carpets in hidden cornérs. _ 3
As in the early ages the Gaelic missionaries carried
to the Continent their idea of a hostel, might we not
now repay to ourselves the debt and take from France
or Ttaly the idea of a country inn, converfing i into «
character of our own? The Irish inn of our dreams
stands by itself a short distance from the village streef
—a low white-washed wall separates' it from the road,
and behind the wall rises a thick fuchsia hedge. White
dashed stones line on either side the path to the en-
trance. The house walls are also white, and the win-
dow sills are painted black. No flower boxes stand ire
the windows to bar the free passage of the light and
air, bub in a,narrow border stretching on eiflier side of
the house grow sweet smelling plants. The door opens
into o little hall with a floor covering of oil-cloth in
black and white squares. This is washed over once
or twice a week. Opposite the door rises the stairway,
which has a narrow matting up the centre of the
steps. The walls ave white and the woodwork maho-
gany. colour. On the right a door leads nto the sit-
tingroom, there the impression is one of freshness
and brightness. White and blue baineen tweed form
the curtains, the paperless walls are whife, and the
floor, stained and polished, has on it several rugs. Two
or three oak coverless tables, some rush scated chairs,
a few armchairs, and a holland covered sofa complete
the furniture. A bookcase stands in one corner, a few
papers lie on the tables, but there are no magazines
two or three years old. No knick-knacks are:to be
seen anywhere. The mantelpiece is guiitless of dust-
collecting draperies. Plenty of light and air come in
through the windows, which open readily. Near the
fireplace a basket filled with turf and wood speaks of &
fire to those who want one. Opposite to the sittingroom
is the diningroom, it is a perfectly plain room, and
on the long dining table the white cloth is laid only
before each meal and not left on perpetually. A delf
bowl filled with flowers occupies the centre of the table.
The knives and forks—well they do not require a fur-
tive rub with a napkin before use. Upstairs the bed-
rooms are on the same lines. They have carpetless
floors acquainted with the scrubbing brush, a rug or
two. Bright coloured linen counterpanes replace the
heavy quilts and white baineen curtains hang at the
windows. In addition to the necessary furniture there
is a table, strong and of fair dimensions—a thing that
in many inns the traveller sighs for in vain. Nowhere .
are to be seen ghosts of family memories and the guests
do not jostle continually against the proprietor’s trea-
sures. ‘They do not feel they are intruders in other
people’s ‘surroundings, and for the moment can make
the surroundings their own. As to the fafe offered it
has a local flavour. Home-made bread, brown and
white, honey from the hives that can be seen in a row
outside, butter, milk, eggs in profusion, potato-cales
such as only the experienced Bean an Tighe can make,
vegetables of all kinds, juicy little chickens, bacon that
hasnever crossed the seas, jams, bottled fruits that bear
no foreign label—and all of it served with care and
thought.  There is nothing to offend the taste in this
little hostel where all is so simple and so plain. But
everything is good of its kind and of home manufacture.
When shall we see such inns in Ireland? The de-
mand seems to have come for them. Many people
who before wandered abroad now explove their own
country, either to seek its beauties in remote places or
to pick up the language of their forefathers. Hence
the hostels of the byway have their visitors ready if
only they were ready themselves. (@4, 10),

GENERAL business boycott, interfering with the
ordinary course of trade, is a discrimination

made against the traders of a particular country or

locality, on other grounds than business. Questions of

price, quality of goods-and convenience are pub aside

for the moment. to gain a particular end. It is an

economic war in which there are sacrifices and losses on

both sides. It may be dictated for national or for

religious reasons, and in either case there is nothing
against human or divine law in it. It is a legitimate

form of warfare well known and put in practice even in
countries supposed to be unprogressive. When Ben-
gal was about to be partitioned against the wishes of
the people a general boycott was declared on British

goods. Later on, whan the Chinese were displeased
with certain action taken by the United States, a
general boycott was declared by Chinese merchants on
American goods. Every strike of the working classes
for higher wages is of the nature of a boycott in which
present losses are borne for the sake of future gain.
The prohibitive tariffs of certain foreign countries are
a partial boycott of foreign manufactures for the pro-
tection of native industries. Here again sacrifices are
made by the consumers of foreign goods for the sake
of the general welfare of the country. Now for some
time past it is pretty well known that a boycott of
Belfast goods is being carried out in the South and
West. Captain Craig brought under the notice of the
House of Commons a ‘“black list”” he got hold of,
which was being circulated among Catholic traders and
was made up of the names of a number of Belfast
Unionist firms. It also, it appears, gave cer-
tain hints about not dealing with such. Whether the
list had much to do with the growth of the boycott is
hard to say. The natural supposition is that the
movement sprang up naturally as a result of

Belfast Orangemen, with all the shameful history of
his savage and intolerant Society behind him and”the
present-day bigotry of his city making it a byword over
Tiurope and America. The insincerity of the article is
apparent from the fact of its never mentioning Religion,
as if there was question of Politics only, though the
world knows that hatred of Religion is at the bottom of
Belfast intolerance, and is the key to its political
action and extravagances.

In the following extract the cloven foot appeats:

‘“The ordinary business procedure of the country
was interfered with in order that a wholly unjustifi-
able and savage crusade should be waged against
an entire city, because of the misconduct, real or
alleged, of a few who had in a moment of exaspera-
tion lost self-control and behaved badly.’’

This is the usual Belfast palliation of the outrages
which drove from their work more than 2,000 Catholic
workers and of numberless other savage instances of
intolerance that we hear nothing about. It is amusing
to note the enormous difference of the measure applied
by Northern self-righteousness to Southern shortcom-
ings from that meted out to its own delinquencies. It
may be hailed as the most recent exemplification of the
Parable of the mote in the brother’s eye. The refusal
of the Catholic traders to deal with Belfast firms in
retaliation for the outrages is an '‘ unjustifiable and
savage crusade,” while the outrages themselves, which
have aroused the indignation of all right-minded men
outside the bigoted city, are explained away as ‘‘ the
misconduct, real or alleged, of a few in a moment of
exasperation.’’

The article has another surprise for us. It furnishes
us with a lesson in toleration—from where do you think ?
—from the much maligned city of Belfast itself. So
now, ye Southern traders, listen to this: 3

‘“ They (the Belfast Unionist employers) exhibit
their breadth of mind and toleration by continuing
to give employment to thousands of Nationalists,

THE LEADER.

509

notwithstanding the black list and notwithstanding:
the fact that their representatives have been re-
fused business.’’ 9774 <
Tlirst of all, I should like to see this fact verified in
its entirety, for I have heard of numbers of quiet dis-
missals having taken place within the last few months,.
by reason rather of the Covenant than of the Boycott.
However, grant that there have been no dismissals,.
what does the argument amount to? Because, for-
sooth, the Protestant employers have not immediately
dismissed in a fit of rage their Catholic employees, on
being refused business in the South and West, ‘ they
oxhibit breadth of mind and toleration.” A sorry
argument, indeed, but I suppose the best that can be
brought forward to malke black look white. Belfast in-
tolerance is too large -and complicated a system to be
disposed of so easily. It is not the growth of a day, nor
confined to a single class. It runs through all the parts
of the body politic. How is it that the Catholics off
Belfast are mostly engaged in the lowest forms of
labour? How is it that all the rich employers are Pro-
testants?  How is it that all the warehouses, large
shops and factories are in the hands of Protestants, and'
that the Catholics employed in them occupy only the
lowest positions? It is accounted for by the systematic
intolerance carried on for generations against the
*‘ Papishes.”’ Catholics have never had any chance
in commercial life in the Northern Babylon.” What-
ever their talents or industry or long service, they could
not hope to rise to a commanding position in the firms
run by men so distinguished for “* breadth of mind and
toleration.”” Hence their general poverty and abject
dependence on Protestant masters at the present day.
The anomalous commercial ascendancy of Belfast,
taken in conjunction with its open hostility to every-
thing National and Catholic, should be a subject of
serious thought for Catholic traders throughout the
country. This home of bigotry, alien in its ways of
thought to Ireland and alien even to the present cen-
tury, has succeeded in making itself a great distribut-
ing centre of English and Scotch wares for a large ares
of the Catholic provinces which could just as easily be
warehoused in and distributed through Dublin. ~ Its
commercial prosperity depends on the goodwill of the:
Catholic Provinces, while they are quite independent of
Belfast. That fact ought to be fully appreciated by our
people. The writer of the article in the *“ Belfast Even-
ing Telegraph " speaks of coercion brought on shop-
keepers not to deal with Northern travellers, under
threats of a boycott of customers, and complains of
this as ‘‘ unjust, un-Trish and unbusinesslike.” Tt
would be hard to prove that any coercion of the kind
has talen place, but if it has it could be defended. The
shopkeeping class is in all countries the most upright,
stable and virtuous of the population. But it does not:
see far and is inclined to look to present gains too
exclusively.  The justice of the boyeott has been
already dealt with. As to its being 3

un-Trish "’ we
cannotl but smile at seeing that plea come from a Bel-
fast Unionist.  That it is ‘‘ unbusinesslike >’ is true,
but businegs prineiples have often to be put aside for the
nonce in the pursuit of national or religious ideals.

If Belfast continues with stupid obstinacy to flout the
aspirations of the people of Ireland and to treat the
Catholics who have their home in it as helots, it must
expect retaliation. The recent outrages have had the
good effect of opening the eyes of our people, who are
beginning to feel their growing strength, to the deplor~
able state of things in that city as regards their co-
religionists. Tt would not be at all surprising if the
boycott, begun on such just grounds, ‘gradually
effected a large transfer of business to the metropolis,.

On SUNDAY, I12ith JANUARY, 1913

THE ANNUAL CHARITY SERMON in aid of the above
Institution, will be preached in the Church of St. Francis Xavier,
Upper Gardiner Street, by the Rev. Robert Kane, S.J.; and on
the same day, also after the First Gospel of the Last Mass,
another Appel Il be made in the Franciscan Capuchins’ Church,
Church Street, by the Very Rev. Father Angelus, O.S.F.C., for
the same benevolent purpose. The object for which the Sisters of
Our Lady of Charity of Refuge now: appeal is one they feel
assured which commends itself to all Christian hearts, and which _
will not fail to awaken their sympathy, and to win their cordial
co-operation. It is for the great purpose of enabling the Sisters
to maintain an Asylum, a refuge, a sanctuary for the poorest, the
most affiicted and the most abandoned of our fellow-creatures.
Our Divine Redeemer taught us that Charity went hand-in-hand
with every other virtue. He who rescued cne little lamb of the
true Sheepfold from spiritual death, ‘ he availeth his own soul.””
What, then, would it be when the hand of charity would be
extended to those who were sunk in the very depths of misery
and distress, and when that charity imparted to them the greatest
blessings, the greatest mercies! The poor fallen Magdalen had
once a happy home—a home of peace, a home of joy; but the
orodigal child had entered on dangerous paths, and now the world

ated her with scorn, and every door to peace and comfort was

against her, even the paternal home. Hopeless would be the
i of the poor Magdalen if there was no refuge where she might
be brought to the Sacréd Feet of the loving Good Shepherd of
souls, whose Divine Heart yearned to take that child to her
previous state ‘of grace and Christian life into His saving fold.
The Asylum, which is the largest in the City of Dublin, now
shelters within its walls 216 poor Magdalens. To maintain these
in sickness and in health the Sisters have no regular fund of any.
kind—their trust is in God and in the charity of their friends and
benefactors, and the generous public. Not only for the Magdalens
who are already safe in the Asylum do the sisters ask sympathy,
but for the many applicants who, weary to death of sin and its
attendant misery and degradation, come daily to their gate praying
for admission. ~Confidently, therefore, the Sisters lift an humble
voice in behalf of a work which has a sanction all divine, and is
hallowed by an example than which there is none more beautiful
in the life of Him who came to call not the just, but sinners to
repentance.  Give for the sake of the Sacred Heart of Jesus
Christ, who emphasised one sorrow and one pain in His agony,
and it was thirst—a thirst not for the water of -y earthly spring,
but a thirst for the souls He was dying # save. The Holy
Sacralice of the Ma nd the daily prar_.s of the Community
and poor Magdalens, arc offered for the Benefactors, both living
and dead. Donations from those who cannot attend the Sermon
will be thankfully received by His Grace the Most Rev, Dr. Walsh;
the Rev. Preachers; the Rev. Fathers of Gardiner Street and
Church Street ; the Clergymen of the Parish and City ; Dr. Murphy,
Physician to the Institution; and the Mother Superior of the
Convent. »
